name: VISION.md
title: Vision
body:
# Vision

> Source: [docs/phasekeep-proposal.md](phasekeep-proposal.md) §1–§4, §8, §13,
> §18 — the specification of record (D-002) — sharpened by author direction
> recorded as D-011, D-012, D-013. This is the distillate; on disagreement the
> proposal-plus-those-decisions wins.

## What This Is

**PhaseKeep is a project management system meets workflow management system
for agent-driven development, at the quality bar of the best local consoles
(codex-class session consoles, t3.chat-class speed, Linear-class keyboard
ergonomics)** — built on Clauderizer memory (D-013). Its tickets are gameplans
and phases; its workers are AI agents; its ground truth is the durable,
structured memory those agents read and write.

Clauderizer made agent memory durable, but working with it today is a
cumbersome, terminal-bound experience: rich state legible only one file at a
time, a large vocabulary of powerful operations that newcomers cannot
discover, agents mutating memory invisibly while they run. The operator of a
multi-agent workflow is flying on instruments that do not exist yet. PhaseKeep
builds those instruments — and the good user experience the underlying system
deserves.

Three capabilities, strictly ordered by value-to-cost:

1. **The window** — a live dashboard over Clauderizer state (portfolio,
   gameplans, open items, invariants, decisions, the dependency graph,
   lessons, docs) across all your projects at once.
2. **The hands** — an embedded cross-platform terminal (bash/zsh, PowerShell,
   cmd, WSL distros) in the same surface.
3. **The crew** — the heterogeneous agent fleet: claude, kimi, grok, local
   models behind any supported harness, each an **(agent, host)** profile on
   one roster, routed to phases by assignment, launched, supervised,
   reattached, and dispatched — with every decision driven by memory state,
   never by interpreting agent output.

It ships as a signed native desktop app (Windows/macOS/Linux, out-of-box WSL
support) with `npx phasekeep` as the secondary developer channel on the same
core.

## See → Understand → React (D-011, D-012)

The dashboard is not a passive pane of glass. The product's core interaction
loop:

- **See.** State that only files could show becomes visual: the dependency
  graph is *witnessed*, not grepped — entities, semver-pinned edges, cascade
  results overlaid; the portfolio shows phase progress, baselines, and
  pending-cascade badges at a glance.
- **Understand.** The Clauderizer lexicon (gameplan, phase, cascade,
  pre-flight, handoff, invariant, lesson, …) is a set of words bound to
  machinery, and PhaseKeep teaches it in place: every domain term in every
  view carries a plain-english hint in the clauderizer.com register, and
  vocabulary chips beside the terminal input stage the operative phrases
  ("cascade that change", "write the handoff") with one-line explanations —
  chips stage text, they never execute. Users learn the language by using it,
  not by reading a glossary.
- **React.** Every surfaced condition carries its reaction, split by shape:
  mechanical reactions are one-op buttons (check a criterion, resolve an
  item, *ask for the cascade* a pending change deserves); judgment-shaped
  reactions (verdicts, close-outs, rituals) dispatch an agent to do the work.
  A checkpoint button on any live session asks its agent, by staged prompt,
  to record where things stand — input in, output never. Seeing a problem
  and answering it are the same gesture, and the judgment stays where it
  belongs — with agents, never UI-authored logic.

## The Operator's Day

The product exists for a specific working life: several projects, several
agents, all day.

Morning: open PhaseKeep and read the **reconciliation pass like a newspaper**
— what completed overnight, what stalled, what tripped a chain-breaker, what
drifted (gameplans never closed out, criteria left unchecked) — each finding
with its reaction attached. During the day: **mission control** — dispatch
agents against phases from their assignments, watch memory change live while
sessions run (supervision, not after-the-fact auditing), intervene cheaply by
taking over any pty, run your own shells in the same tabs. The terminal
sprawl of PowerShell-here-WSL-there is gone; every terminal is associated
with its project, agent, and gameplan. Overnight: **hands-off autonomy**,
opt-in rung by rung — continue fires only on memory-state evidence (the
phase actually transitioned, with process exit or detected state-quiescence
as the liveness gate — never anything an agent printed); attempt counters,
wall-clock budgets, and standing-condition chain-breakers bound the blast
radius; loops crank loop-kind gameplans under daily caps. The UI is the pager and the journal is
the overnight history — no notifications, no telemetry, agents running as
you, stated bluntly.

The signature interaction compresses all of it into one screen: **creating a
gameplan opens a terminal.** The plus-button launches the assigned (or
manager) agent in a pty with the planning prompt staged; the planning
negotiation runs right there while the portfolio pane live-populates beside
it — words becoming recorded decisions and phases in real time (D-010; the
designated Milestone 4 demo).

## Differentiation

- **The memory layer is the defensible territory.** First-party agent tools
  render *their* sessions; they will not render *Clauderizer's memory* — the
  DAG, the portfolio, staleness-aware approvals, the drift.
- **The contract is the real product.** PhaseKeep never parses Clauderizer's
  files; it consumes a versioned JSON read contract (Milestone 0). Anyone can
  build a TUI, an editor extension, or a CI check on the same surface —
  PhaseKeep is the reference client, not the privileged one.
- **Restraint as architecture.** Agents are PTYs with metadata, not parsed
  processes; completion is process exit plus state transition. This is what
  keeps one part-time maintainer out of an orchestration arms race (H-01).
- **An adoption asset for Clauderizer.** Memory you can *see* — and a
  vocabulary you can learn by touch — is dramatically easier to trust,
  debug, and demo. Brand posture: PhaseKeep is the product; Clauderizer is
  infrastructure (§15.2 defines the concealment boundary and its three honest
  limits).

## Audience (D-013)

**Operator-first, teach-ready.** v1 is the author's own daily instrument
first — that is what keeps it honest — but the vocabulary layer makes it
learnable by any Clauderizer newcomer from day one. Education is core UX, not
an onboarding afterthought; it just never outranks the daily-driver features.

## Scope Boundaries (v1 non-goals)

1. **No interpreting agent output** — the fence (INVARIANT-02). State-based
   routing/dispatch is in; anything requiring the UI to understand what an
   agent printed is out.
2. **No remote or multi-user access** — loopback only; the remote story is an
   SSH tunnel (INVARIANT-03).
3. **No free-form writes** — narrow form-shaped write classes through the ops
   registry (INVARIANT-10: one-op mechanical actions
   including advisory report generation, scaffolding, assignments);
   everything judgment-shaped is dispatched agent work.
4. **No second first-class form factor** — app primary, `npx` tab secondary.
5. **No mobile.**
6. **No native-Windows persistent sessions** — persistence requires WSL
   (tmux); native Windows gets ephemeral sessions, labeled honestly.
7. **No telemetry, ever** (INVARIANT-04). The sole outbound call is the
   disableable installer update check.
8. **No heavy PM surfaces** (D-013) — no kanban, timelines, or workload
   views until an operator need proves them; the 8.1 views plus light
   progress boards are the PM system.

## Success Criteria (§18)

1. The operator answers "what is pending, what are the agents doing, what are
   the invariants" from one surface, in seconds, across all active projects,
   daily.
2. Zero silent agent-session loss on Tier 1 platforms.
3. At least one external client consumes the read contract within six months
   of announcement.
4. Maintenance stays one-person-side-project sized: issues driven by Tier 1
   features, not install failures.
5. Clauderizer adoption measurably benefits — and its vocabulary stops being
   a barrier, because the UI teaches it.

## Execution Shape

Six gameplans map the proposal's milestones: `m0-read-contract` →
`m1-dashboard` → `m2-terminal` → `m3-agent-sessions` → `m35-installers` →
`m4-announcement` (D-001). Each ends independently valuable; a pause after
any strands nothing. The D-011/D-012/D-013 sharpenings are folded into m1
Phases 2/3/5/6 and m2 Phase 3.

Said out loud: PhaseKeep is deliberately built *through* Clauderizer memory —
its own development is the engine's reference workload and the product's
first dogfood. The process apparatus (gameplans, handoffs, cascades) is
therefore partly the point, not pure overhead; the guard is that process
artifacts earn their keep by changing decisions or handing off context,
never ceremony for its own sake.
